metadata.title: The Gap Between the Model and the System: Ameya Padwad's Work
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Ameya Padwad
tags: The Gap Between the Model and the System
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Ameya Padwad — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Ameya Padwad learned early that a working model and a working system are not the same thing. His key insight came from the warehouse floor, making it earned rather than borrowed. Judges found his work not only technically sound but practically compelling, which is the harder test. His PyTorch neural network for AI content detection reached 83% accuracy, beating both Logistic Regression and Random Forest.

artifactLines: Ameya Padwad learned early that a working model and a working system are not the same thing.
His key insight came from the warehouse floor, making it earned rather than borrowed.
Judges found his work not only technically sound but practically compelling, which is the harder test.
His PyTorch neural network for AI content detection reached 83% accuracy, beating both Logistic Regression and Random Forest.

beats.narration_text: Ameya Padwad's insight that the gap lives between the model and the system, earned from the warehouse rather than borrowed, and his PyTorch detector hitting 83% against Logistic Regression and Random Forest, makes me want to reproduce his comparison. Can you (1) explain what a warehouse-earned insight about deployment gaps actually looks like in code versus in a notebook, (2) set up the same three-way benchmark of a neural net, Logistic Regression, and Random Forest for AI-content detection, and (3) show why 83% was the strongest result and where the other two lose ground?
